title: Intraabdominal abscesses
preambleMarkdown:
# Intraabdominal abscesses



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Intraabdominal abscesses is a localized collection of pus that usually requires source control when mature or clinically significant.
- Intraabdominal abscesses is an infectious syndrome or organism-related topic in infectious diseases.
- This article focuses on organism, exposure, syndrome, diagnostics, antimicrobial selection, isolation, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Intraabdominal abscesses to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Intraabdominal abscesses is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Community-acquired, healthcare-associated, travel-related, device-related, or opportunistic infection.
- Localized, disseminated, recurrent, latent, or postinfectious presentation.
- Bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, toxin-mediated, or mixed infection.
- Uncomplicated disease, severe disease, sepsis, source-control need, or outbreak/public-health concern.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Intraabdominal abscesses depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In infectious diseases, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Intraabdominal abscesses.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Intraabdominal abscesses disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Abscess formation reflects infection plus inflammatory walling-off, which limits antimicrobial penetration.
- The central mechanism in Intraabdominal abscesses should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Intraabdominal abscesses first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Intraabdominal abscesses, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever, focal pain, tenderness, swelling, erythema, fluctuance, drainage, or sepsis may occur depending on location.
- Deep abscesses may present with persistent fever, leukocytosis, organ dysfunction, or vague localizing symptoms.
- Fever, localizing symptoms, exposure history, immune status, device or procedure history, and severity define the syndrome.
- Immunocompromised patients, neonates, pregnancy, and older adults may lack classic symptoms.
- Sepsis, airway threat, neurologic involvement, necrosis, and rapidly progressive disease change urgency.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Intraabdominal abscesses, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused infectious diseases examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Intraabdominal abscesses presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Intraabdominal abscesses with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use bedside examination for superficial collections and ultrasound, CT, or MRI for deep, complex, or anatomically risky collections.
- First decide whether Intraabdominal abscesses is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Intraabdominal abscesses should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Drainage is usually the key treatment when a drainable collection is present.
- Antibiotics are added for systemic illness, surrounding cellulitis, immunocompromise, difficult anatomy, incomplete drainage, or high-risk host features.
- Stabilize sepsis, obtain high-yield cultures when they will not delay care, and start empiric therapy when indicated.
- Narrow therapy once organism, susceptibility, source, and response are known.
- Add source control, isolation, prophylaxis, vaccination, contact tracing, or public-health notification when relevant.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Intraabdominal abscesses is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Start empiric antimicrobial therapy promptly when severe infection, sepsis, meningitis, necrotizing infection, or immunocompromised infection is suspected.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Drain abscesses, remove infected devices, debride necrotic tissue, decompress obstruction, or obtain diagnostic samples when source control is needed.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Intraabdominal abscesses, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Intraabdominal abscesses may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Intraabdominal abscesses patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Intraabdominal abscesses, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Intraabdominal abscesses in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Intraabdominal abscesses, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Intraabdominal abscesses does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Intraabdominal abscesses may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Intraabdominal abscesses, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Intraabdominal abscesses as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Source control is central.
- Antibiotics alone often fail when a mature drainable abscess is present.
- Culture drained material when results will change therapy.
- Do not delay antimicrobials in unstable sepsis.
- Cultures are most valuable before antibiotics when feasible, but stabilization comes first.
- Source control can matter as much as antimicrobial choice.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Intraabdominal abscesses draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Intra-Abdominal Abscess

_Source lines: 34038-34407._

```text
Intra-Abdominal Abscess
- Causes include spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, pelvic infection (e.g.,
tubo-ovarian abscess), pancreatitis, perforation of the GI tract, and
osteomyelitis of the vertebral bodies with extension into the
retroperitoneal cavity.

- Usually polymicrobial in origin.

- Diagnose using CT scan or ultrasound.

- Treatment typically involves drainage of the abscess.

- The antibiotic regimen should include broad coverage against gram-
negative rods, enterococci, and anaerobes.

Clostridioides dificile Infection (CDI)
A. General Characteristics
1. This is also referred to as pseudomembranous colitis, although not
all patients will have pseudomembranes.

2. Antibiotic treatment kills organisms that normally inhibit growth of
Clostridium dificile, leading to overgrowth of C. dificile and toxin
production.

3. Any antibiotic can predispose to CDI, but the most frequently
implicated antibiotics are fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, broad-
spectrum penicillins, and cephalosporins.

4. Symptoms usually begin within 2 weeks of antibiotic therapy.

However, up to 10 weeks may elapse after stopping antibiotics
before clinical findings become apparent. Community-acquired CDI
can also occur in the absence of recent exposure to antibiotics.

5. Disease severity can be classified as nonsevere, severe, or fulminant
depending on the WBC count, serum creatinine, and presence of
hypotension/shock, ileus, or megacolon.

FIGURE
10-5 Abdominal x-ray showing toxic megacolon in a patient with C. dificile
enterocolitis.

(Reprinted with permission from Marino PL. Marino's The ICU Book: Print + Ebook with
Updates. 4th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2013. Figure 40-3.)

Quick HIT
Complications of severe CDI that may require surgery:
- Toxic megacolon
- Colonic perforation or ischemia

- Abdominal compartment syndrome
- Progressive multiorgan failure
B. Clinical Features
1. Profuse watery diarrhea (>=3 loose stools in 24 hours), usually
without blood or mucus
2. Crampy abdominal pain
3. Toxic megacolon (in severe cases) with risk of perforation (Figure
10-5)
C. Diagnosis
1. A positive C. dificile stool toxin PCR in the presence of diarrhea is
diagnostic. Only liquid stool should be tested (as C. dificile toxin
gene may be detected in the asymptomatic carrier state, which does
not require treatment).

2. Stool anaerobic culture for C. dificile has high sensitivity, but
results take several days.

3. Lower endoscopy is warranted only for selected cases where the
diagnosis remains unclear and direct visualization of the bowel
mucosa is needed.

4. Abdominal radiograph or CT of the abdomen/pelvis is indicated for
severe or fulminant CDI to evaluate for toxic megacolon or other
complications.

5. Leukocytosis is very common.

D. Treatment
1. Discontinue the offending antibiotic, if possible.

2. Supportive care for fluid resuscitation and electrolyte repletion.

3. Initial episode if nonsevere or severe: fidaxomicin or PO
vancomycin. Fidaxomicin is preferred if available.

4. Initial episode if fulminant: PO vancomycin and IV metronidazole.

If ileus is present, consider fecal microbiota transplantation or
rectal vancomycin retention enema. Subtotal colectomy or
diverting loop ileostomy with colonic lavage may be necessary.

5. First recurrence: fidaxomicin or PO vancomycin. If recurrence is
within 6 months of prior infection, offer adjunct bezlotoxumab
(monoclonal antibody that binds C. dificile toxin B) if available.

6. Second or subsequent recurrence: fidaxomicin, PO vancomycin
with long taper, or fetal microbiota transplantation. Offer adjunct
bezlotoxumab if available.

7. Regardless of choice of antibiotic, 15% to 35% of successfully
treated patients will have recurrence within 2 to 8 weeks after
stopping the antibiotic. Adjunct treatment with bezlotoxumab for

recurrent CDI reduces recurrence risk, but has not been shown to
reduce mortality and drug is costly.

Infections of the Genitourinary Tract
Cystitis (Lower Urinary Tract Infections)
A. General Characteristics
1. Note that here and elsewhere in text, the terms "women" and "men" will
be used, but recommendations for women extend to gender-diverse
persons with female external genitalia at birth, and recommendations
for men extend to gender-diverse persons with male external genitalia at
birth.

2. Cystitis is much more common in women than in men. Up to 33%
of all women experience cystitis in their lifetime. The most
common type is acute simple (uncomplicated) cystitis.

3. Simple or uncomplicated cystitis refers to infection limited to the
bladder. When infection has extended beyond the bladder and
there are features suggestive of systemic illness (e.g., fever, chills,
flank pain), this is considered complicated UTI.

4. The majority of cases are caused by ascension of pathogens from
the urethra to the bladder. These pathogens are from fecal flora
that have colonized the area.

5. Common organisms.

a. E. coli (most common)-causes 80% of cases.

b. Other organisms-Staphylococcus saprophyticus, Enterococcus,
Klebsiella, Proteus spp., Pseudomonas, Enterobacter, and yeast
(such as Candida spp.)

Quick HIT
More than 90% of uncomplicated cystitis is caused by E. coli, S.

saprophyticus, and Enterococcus spp. A small percentage is
caused by Proteus, Klebsiella, Enterobacter, and Pseudomonas.

B. Risk Factors
1. Female genital anatomy-greater risk due to shorter urethral tract
and distance from anus to urethra
2. Sexual intercourse
a. Often the trigger of cystitis in women

b. Use of diaphragms and spermicides further increases risk (alters
vaginal colonization)

## 3. Pregnancy

4. Indwelling urinary catheters-risk factor for hospitalized patients
5. Personal history of recurrent UTIs
6. Host-dependent factors-increase risk for recurrent or complicated
cystitis
a. Diabetes mellitus
b. Patients with spinal cord injury
c. Immunocompromised state
d. Any structural or functional abnormality that impedes urinary
flow (e.g., incomplete voiding, neurogenic bladder, BPH,
vesicoureteral reflux, calculi)
7. Male risk factors
a. Uncircumcised males are at higher risk due to bacterial
colonization of the foreskin
b. Insertive anal intercourse
c. Vaginal intercourse if colonized with uropathogens
Quick HIT
Noninfectious causes of cystitis or cystitis-like symptoms:
- Cytotoxic agents (e.g., cyclophosphamide)
- Radiation to the pelvis
- Dysfunctional voiding
- Interstitial cystitis
Quick HIT
Urine culture and sensitivity is usually the definitive diagnostic test.

Acute simple cystitis in women can be treated based on urinalysis
and presence of classic signs and symptoms alone.

C. Clinical Features
1. Dysuria-commonly expressed as burning on urination

## 2. Frequency

## 3. Urgency

4. Suprapubic tenderness
5. Gross hematuria is sometimes present
6. In acute simple cystitis, fever and other systemic symptoms
are characteristically absent

Quick HIT
Asymptomatic Bacteriuria
- To diagnose asymptomatic bacteriuria, two successive positive
cultures (>=10 5 CFU/mL) must be present.

- Treat asymptomatic bacteriuria only in pregnancy or before
urologic surgery.

D. Diagnosis
1. Dipstick urinalysis
a. Positive urine leukocyte esterase test-presence of leukocyte
esterase reflects pyuria
b. Positive nitrite test for presence of bacteria (gram-negative)-
nitrite test is sensitive and specific for detecting
Enterobacteriaceae and other nitrite-producing bacteria. But it
lacks sensitivity for other organisms, so a negative test should
be interpreted with caution
c. Combining the above two tests yields a sensitivity of 85% and
specificity of 75%
Quick HIT
On UA, most important finding is white blood cells.

1. Urinalysis (clean-catch midstream specimen)
a. Adequacy of collection
- The presence of epithelial (squamous) cells indicates vulvar
or urethral contamination
- If contamination is suspected, perform a straight
catheterization of the bladder
d. Criteria for UTI (interpret in presence of symptoms, as these
findings in the absence of symptoms only indicate symptomatic
bacteriuria or pyuria)
- Bacteriuria: >=1 organism per oil-immersion field.

Bacteriuria without WBCs may reflect contamination and is
not a reliable indicator of infection
- Pyuria is the most valuable finding for diagnosis: >10
leukocytes/L is abnormal
g. Other findings-hematuria and mild proteinuria may be
present. Hematuria in and of itself does not require extended
therapy

## 2. Urine Gram stain

a. A count of >10 5 organisms/mL represents significant
bacteriuria
b. It is 90% sensitive and 88% specific
3. Urine culture
a. Confirms the diagnosis (high specificity). Obtaining a urine
culture is warranted if symptoms are not characteristic of
cystitis, if a complicated infection is suspected, if antimicrobial
resistance is suspected, or if symptoms persist despite prior
antibiotic treatment
b. Traditional criteria: >=10 5 CFU/mL of urine from a clean-catch
sample; misses up to one-third of UTIs
c. Colony counts as low as 10 3 CFU/mL are adequate for diagnosis
if clinical symptoms are present, especially if at risk for
complicated UTI
4. Blood cultures-only indicated if patient is ill and systemic
infection is suspected
5. IV pyelogram, cystoscopy, and excretory urography are not
recommended unless structural abnormalities or obstruction is
suspected
E. Complications

## 1. Complicated UTI

a. Any UTI that spreads beyond the bladder (e.g., pyelonephritis,
prostatitis, sepsis). Commonly presents with signs of infection
beyond bladder (e.g., fever, chills, flank pain, costovertebral
angle tenderness, perineal pain suggestive of prostatitis)
b. Risk factors for upper UTI: pregnancy, diabetes,
immunocompromising conditions, or urologic abnormalities
2. UTI during pregnancy-increased risk of preterm labor, low birth
weight, and other complications, especially in advanced pregnancy
3. Recurrent infections
a. Usually due to infection with new organism, but sometimes is a
relapse due to unsuccessful treatment of the original organism
b. Risk factors include impaired host defenses, pregnancy,
vesicoureteral reflux, and sexual intercourse
Quick HIT
Complicated UTI refers to infection that has spread beyond the
bladder. Some use the term "complicated UTI" to refer to UTI with
the following factors (although this definition is not widely accepted):
- Men
- Diabetes mellitus
- Renal impairment

- Pregnancy
- Urinary tract obstruction, indwelling catheter, stent,
nephrostomy tube, or recent instrumentation
- Antibiotic-resistant organism
- Recurrent infections
- Immunocompromised state (e.g., HIV, organ transplantation)
F. Treatment
1. Acute uncomplicated cystitis-most commonly in nonpregnant
women. Several options exist:
a. Oral TMP/SMX (Bactrim) for 3 days.

b. Nitrofurantoin (5 to 7 days)-do not give if early pyelonephritis
is suspected.

c. Fosfomycin (single dose)-do not give if early pyelonephritis is
suspected.

d. Amoxicillin-clavulanate, cefpodoxime, or cefadroxil can be used
alternatively if any of the above are not options.

e. Avoid amoxicillin monotherapy due to high prevalence of
antimicrobial resistance.

f. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin in 3-day regimen) is a
reasonable alternative to the above-mentioned agents. Try to
avoid due to higher rates of adverse effects with
fluoroquinolones.

g. Treat presumptively for pyelonephritis if the condition fails to
respond to a short course of antibiotics.

h. Phenazopyridine (Pyridium) is a urinary analgesic; it can be
given for 1 to 3 days for dysuria.

Quick HIT
For acute simple cystitis, empiric treatment is appropriate-do not
wait for culture results.

Quick HIT
If complicated UTI, extend antibiotic treatment to 5 to 7 days.

## 1. Pregnant women with UTI

a. Treat with amoxicillin, foxfomycin, or oral cephalosporins for 7
to 10 days.

b. Avoid fluoroquinolones (can cause fetal arthropathy).

2. UTIs in men

a. Treat as with uncomplicated cystitis in women, but consider
longer duration (5 to 7 days) if concern for complicated UTI.

b. Urologic workup is recommended in men with recurrent UTI,
including evaluation for prostatic hypertrophy or other urinary
tract obstruction.

3. Recurrent infections
a. If relapse occurs within 3 months, check a urine culture to
evaluate for antimicrobial resistance.

b. Otherwise treat as for uncomplicated cystitis. If the patient has
two or more UTIs within 6 months, consider antibiotic
prophylaxis. However, must weigh risk of adverse effects and
selection for resistant bacteria.

- Postcoital prophylaxis in women with recurrent UTI: single
dose of TMP/SMX or nitrofurantoin after intercourse or at
first signs of symptoms.

- Alternative low-dose prophylactic antibiotics (e.g., low-dose
TMP/SMX) for 3-month trial.
```
